name: Doppler effect — approaching source
question_text: You are standing still and an ambulance siren moves toward you at constant speed. Compared with the sound emitted by the siren, what do you hear?
general_feedback: When a source moves toward a stationary observer, successive wave fronts are emitted from closer positions, shortening the observed wavelength and raising the observed frequency (higher pitch). After the source passes, the pitch drops.
correct_feedback: Correct — Approaching source → compressed wavefronts → higher observed frequency.

answers.text: A higher pitch than the emitted sound.
answers.feedback: Approaching source → compressed wavefronts → higher observed frequency.

answers.text: A lower pitch than the emitted sound.
answers.feedback: That applies to a receding source, not an approaching one.
Loudness may change with distance but the perceived frequency also shifts; pitch is not unchanged.

answers.text: No sound until the ambulance reaches you.
answers.feedback: Sound propagates through the air continuously; the observer hears the siren throughout its approach.
